title: Haplopappus validus
intro: Haplopappus validus, also known as the Rocky Mountain goldenweed, is a perennial plant species native to the western United States and Canada, characterized by its yellow flowers and taproot system. The plant is valued for its medicinal properties, which include anti-inflammatory, antiseptic, and antiviral effects, making it useful in treating wounds, digestive issues, and respiratory problems. The bioactive constituents of Haplopappus validus include flavonoids, sesquiterpene lactones, and polyphenolic acids, which contribute to its therapeutic properties. The plant is commonly used in herbal preparations such as teas, infusions, and tinctures, often in combination with other herbs to enhance its medicinal effects.
taxonomy: Haplopappus validus is classified within the family Asteraceae, which is a large and widespread group of flowering plants commonly known as the sunflower or daisy family. This family consists of over 23,000 species and is one of the largest and most diverse plant families in the world. The genus Haplopappus is a group of flowering plants that are native to western North America, and Haplopappus validus is one of the species within this genus. Haplopappus validus is further classified as a perennial herb, which means it grows and reproduces year after year. The specific classification of Haplopappus validus is Haplopappus validus (Nutt.) Gray.
